coredata.dc:title: Bio-inspired green fluorescent gold-naringin nanoclusters as a dual-functional optical probe for bio-imaging and intracellular sensing applications
coredata.dc:creator: Sangubotla, Roopkumar
Anantha Lakshmi, Buddolla
Kim, Sanghyo
Kim, Jongsung
coredata.dc:description: Human exposure to mercury (Hg2+) happens in everyday life via exposure to aquatic animals like fishes, shrimps, and prawns. We developed bio-inspired gold-naringin nanoclusters (GNNCs) as a novel fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ ions. GNNCs exhibit green-color emission under UV illumination with excellent selectivity for Hg2+ over other metal ions. GNNCs demonstrated excellent photostability and excitation-dependent emission behaviors. As-synthesized GNNCs facilitate Hg2+ detection in the range of 0–5 µM with a detection limit of 26.42 nM. GNNCs also provide effective multi-color fluorescence imaging of shrimp as an animal model. GNNCs displayed excellent intracellular fluorescence sensing of Hg2+ and in vitro cytotoxicity in cancer (A549) cells. The practicality and feasibility of GNNCs were examined using real water samples from streams and tap water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold-naringin nanoclusters
Hg2+
Multi-color imaging
Shrimps
Detection limit
Intracellular fluorescence sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and purification of green fluorescent GNNCs
Intracellular sensing of Hg2+ using GNNCs in A549 cells
Bio-imaging of shrimps and A549 cells
Optical characterization and functional group analysis of GNNCs
PL dependence on pH and long-term PL stability of GNNCs
Sensitivity and selectivity studies of GNNCs with other metal ions
Salt and oxidative effects of GNNCs
Intracellular sensing of Hg2+ and in vitro imaging using GNNCs
In vivo fluorescence imaging of shrimp

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning
Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank the Smart Materials Research Center for IoT at Gachon University for its instrumental support (FT-IR, UV-Vis spectroscopy, and SEM). This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF2017R1D1A1B04035070) and by the Human Resource Development of the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) and the Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy (MOTIE) of the Republic of Korea (20174030201530).

We thank the Smart Materials Research Center for IoT at Gachon University for its instrumental support (FT-IR, UV-Vis spectroscopy, and SEM). This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education ( NRF2017R1D1A1B04035070 ) and by the Human Resource Development of the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) and the Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy (MOTIE) of the Republic of Korea ( 20174030201530 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Low-resolution TEM image of GNNCs. The inset shows the Gaussian particle size distribution for GNNCs, (b, c) HRTEM image of GNNCs and corresponding Fast Fourier Transformation (FFT) image of an individual nanocluster, and (d) XRD pattern of the GNNCs.
EDX analysis of GNNCs with (a-g) representing carbon (C), oxygen (O), sodium (Na), chlorine (Cl), gold (Au), nitrogen (N), and sulfur (S), respectively. (h) SEM image of GNNCs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of naringin (black line) and GNNCs (red line), (b) PL excitation (black curve) and emission spectra (red curve) of GNNCs, (c) excitation-dependent emission spectra of GNNCs at different excitation wavelengths (300–500 nm), and (d) FT-IR spectra of (i) L-GSH, (ii) naringin, (iii) GNNCs, and (iv) GNNCs treated with Hg2+ ions.

(a) XPS survey spectra of (i) GNNCs (ii) GNNCs treated with Hg2+ ions, (b-g) high-resolution spectra of C 1s, O1s, N 1s, S 2p, Cl 2p, and Au 4f of GNNCs (i) without and (ii) with Hg2+ ion treatment, and (h) Hg 4f high resolution spectrum.

(a) PL emission spectra of GNNCs with various Hg2+ concentrations (0–5 µM), (b) Linear plot between (F0-F/F0) and Hg2+ ion concentrations, (c) Selectivity test of GNNCs on Hg2+ sensing with other metal ions. The concentration was 5 μM for Hg2+and 50 μM for other metal ions. Inset represents the corresponding fluorescence intensity photographs of all interferants (alphabets from a-o). (d) PL emission spectra of GNNCs with various NaCl concentrations (0–2 mol L−1).
Schematic representation for the plausible mechanism of PL quenching of GNNCs by Hg2+ ions.
Intracellular sensing of A549 cells with GNNCs. The bright-field, fluorescent, and overlay images of GNNC-injected A549 cells (A) without Hg2+ as control, (B) with 2.5 μM and (C) with 5 μM of Hg2+.

Laser scanning confocal imaging of A549 cells treated with GNNCs at different excitation wavelengths. (A) Bright field, (B) blue channel (405 nm), (C) green channel (488 nm), and (D) red channel (543 nm).
Confocal fluorescent microscope images of (A) head, (B) body, and (C) tail of shrimp using GNNCs. The excitation wavelength was 405 nm (Blue), 488 nm (Green), and 543 nm (Red). Scale bar: 100 μm.
The photographs of GNNC-injected shrimps (A) without and (B) with Hg2+ ion treatment under UV irradiation (365 nm).
Schematic illustration of synthesis of GNNCs by magnetic stirring (conditions: 37 °C, 4 h, pH 9).
Comparison of different reported gold-mediated nanomaterials to determine Hg2+.

row.entry: Gold Nanoparticles on Paper-Based Analytical Devices

head.ce:title: Bio-inspired green fluorescent gold-naringin nanoclusters as a dual-functional optical probe for bio-imaging and intracellular sensing applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, 1342 Seongnam Daero, Seongnam-Si, Gyeonggi-Do 13120, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, 1342 Seongnam Daero, Seongnam-Si, Gyeonggi-Do 13120, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bionanotechnology, Gachon University, Seongnam 461-701, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Bionanotechnology, Gachon University, Seongnam 461-701, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The gold-naringin nanoclusters (GNNCs) displayed potential applications including intracellular sensing of Hg2+ in A549 cells with multi-color imaging and in vivo optical imaging of shrimp. As-synthesized GNNCs exhibit better biocompatibility and excellent photostability features. These fluorescent probes can further investigate the sensing of Hg2+ with a low detection limit of 26.42 nM over a linear range of 0–5 µM.

Human exposure to mercury (Hg2+) happens in everyday life via exposure to aquatic animals like fishes, shrimps, and prawns. We developed bio-inspired gold-naringin nanoclusters (GNNCs) as a novel fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ ions. GNNCs exhibit green-color emission under UV illumination with excellent selectivity for Hg2+ over other metal ions. GNNCs demonstrated excellent photostability and excitation-dependent emission behaviors. As-synthesized GNNCs facilitate Hg2+ detection in the range of 0–5 µM with a detection limit of 26.42 nM. GNNCs also provide effective multi-color fluorescence imaging of shrimp as an animal model. GNNCs displayed excellent intracellular fluorescence sensing of Hg2+ and in vitro cytotoxicity in cancer (A549) cells. The practicality and feasibility of GNNCs were examined using real water samples from streams and tap water.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently there have been many efforts to cope with environmental damages due to heavy metal pollutants that lead to serious challenges for both human and aquatic animal life [1]. A sensitive and selective detection method for heavy metal ions in aqueous solutions and aquatic animals is greatly required for this reason. Mercury, a well-known heavy metal, is severely toxic to human health, causing allergic reactions in the brain, and many other metabolic disorders [2,3]. Currently, several methods have been reported to detect mercury including atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [4], X-ray fluorescence spectrometry [5], inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS) [6], anodic stripping voltammetry [7], chromatography [8], colorimetry [9–11], and neutron activation analysis [12]. However, all these techniques have significant drawbacks like complicated sample preparation, high equipment cost, and time-consuming detection processes. In the past two decades, various types of metal nanoclusters (NCs) including gold (Au), silver (Ag), and copper (Cu) have gained attention owing to their various applications. Among them, gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) become important for sensing, catalysis, cancer therapy, biomedical imaging, and bio-labeling applications because of their outstanding features like ultra-small size (<2 nm), biocompatibility, size-dependent photoluminescence, and size tunability [13–17]. Recently, the Au NCs have been used as fluorescent probes to detect various heavy metals owing to their large Stokes shift, minimal toxicity, and long-term fluorescence stability [18–20]. Interestingly, highly fluorescent Au NCs, stabilized by DNA, proteins (bovine serum albumin, insulin, pepsin, and horseradish peroxidase), and polymers (dendrimers, polyacrylic acid), have been reported [21,22]. Recently, glutathione (GSH), a well-known antioxidant, has been used as an eco-friendly protecting/reducing agent for the synthesis of highly stabilized Au NCs [23–26]. Flavonoids are plant-derived secondary metabolites with many benefits. They can be used as excellent reducing agents to replace toxic chemicals like sodium borohydride (NaBH4) and hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O) in the conversion of metal ions into respected nanoparticles. In addition, their polar functional groups (including amine and hydroxyl groups) facilitate high aqueous solubility and easy conjugation with metals [27,28]. In this regard, naringin, a significant nutraceutical and flavanone glycoside derived from naringenin (flavanone) and neohesperidose (disaccharide), which is widely available in citrus like grapefruits. It exerts many advantages including antitumor, antioxidant, anti-allergic, and antimicrobial activities. Singh et al. synthesized gold-naringin nanoparticles to detect aluminum metal ions [29–33]. Till now, several methods already have been reported for the intracellular sensing and imaging of Hg2+. However, they have their major hurdles like high complexity and expensiveness [34–37]. The key roles of GSH and BSA playing in the formation of GNNCs are as a reducing and capping agent, respectively. However, BSA is not a dominant reducing agent, it plays as a scaffolding agent for stabilizing the GSH-Au complexes and further, able to protect the GNNCs through its protein nature. Noteworthy, under basic conditions, BSA can mediate the formation of AuNCs owing to the histidine residues well-coordinated with Au3+ ions. In addition, tyrosine residues present in the BSA can efficiently reduce Au3+ ions to generate AuNCs. In this way, we have selected basic pH 9 for the synthesis of GNNCs in the present report. Whereas, addition of GSH in the GNNCs can facilitates the minimal amounts of BSA. This provides the cost effectiveness and ease the purification process in the as-obtained GNNCs [38,39]. On the other hand, plenty of phenolic groups in naringin can facilitate the easy formation of GNNCs. Herein, naringin act as a reducing agent, thereby synergistic effect of all three reagents i.e. GSH, naringin, and BSA involved in the formation of green fluorescent Au NCs. Recently, green color gold nanoclusters are playing enough role in the bio-imaging disciplines. In this regard, Sangita et al. synthesized green emitting gold nanoclusters which have demonstrated intracellular temperature sensing and bio-imaging properties. Purine-stabilized green color gold nanoclusters were developed for the purpose of imaging of nuclei in HeLa cells. H. Li et al. reported growth hormone releasing peptide-6 (GHRP-6) mediated green fluorescent gold nanoclusters for sensing and bio-imaging of Fe3+ [40–42]. However, few limitations must be addressed in the above reports such as expensiveness and complex procedures. We considered the significance of the development of green color gold nanoclusters in the bio-imaging and sensing processes. Herein, we report the synthesis and applications of the green-color emissive GNNCs in both intracellular Hg2+ sensing and bio-imaging of A549 cancer cell lines and shrimps as in vitro and in vivo platforms, respectively. The GNNCs show remarkable photostability, multicolor-imaging, and biocompatibility, allowing us to monitor hazardous Hg2+. Further, we successfully employed GNNCs to detect Hg2+ in real water samples from streams and tap water, showing their practicality and feasibility.

Gold (III) chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), naringin, mercury nitrate monohydrate (Hg (NO3)2·H2O), L-glutathione (GSH), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), iron (II) chloride (FeCl2), iron (III) chloride (FeCl3), copper (II) chloride (CuCl2), and aluminum (III) chloride (AlCl3) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Shrimp samples were acquired from a local market in Seongnam city, South Korea and cleaned using DI (deionized) water. BSA was obtained from Millipore LEE, South Korea. MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay kit was obtained from Bio Vision Milpitas, USA. Fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin-streptomycin solutions, and trypsin-EDTA were purchased from Gibco Laboratories, Korea. Cell culture wares were obtained from NEST Scientific, USA. DI water was used in the experiments. All chemicals used were analytical grade and employed without purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and purification of green fluorescent GNNCs
ce:section.ce:para: GNNCs were synthesized according to the reported method with slight modifications [43]. Before starting the experiment, all glassware was cleansed with an aqua regia (HCl: HNO3 = 3:1, v/v). Briefly, 5 mL of HAuCl4 (10 mM) solution and naringin (20 mg) were mixed in a beaker. 5 mL of BSA solution (50 mg. mL−1) and GSH (100 mM) were added with vigorous stirring. After 10 min, NaOH (0.5 mL, 1 M) was added drop wise to regulate the pH to 9, and further mixture was incubated for 4 h at 37 °C with magnetic stirring. The color of the solution was turned from light yellow to brown, indicating nanocluster formation as illustrated in Scheme 1 . The solution was dialyzed using DI water for 48 h by employing a cellulose ester membrane (molecular weight cut-off: 2 kDa) to remove unreacted naringin and HAuCl4. Finally, the samples were freeze-dried and used for further experiments.

Solutions containing 100 μL of Hg2+ and 400 μL of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) were added to a cuvette containing 100 μL of GNNCs (0.27 mg L−1). The solution was incubated for 15 min, and the photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded with an excitation wavelength at 394 nm. The fluorescence quenching of GNNCs with various concentrations of Hg2+ was studied in the wavelength range between 450 and 650 nm. The maximum PL emission of GNNCs was observed at 514 nm.

Human lung cancer cell lines (A549) and human lung normal cells (WI-38) were cultured according to American Type Culture Collection (ATCC) guidelines in Dulbecco’s Minimum Essential Medium (DMEM) and 10% FBS with 1% penicillin-streptomycin solution. The cells were incubated in a 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C and sub-cultured until reaching 70–80% confluency.

A549 and WI-38 cells were cultured in a 96-well plate and incubated at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 24 h to reach 70% confluency. The medium was removed, and fresh medium was added along with various GNNC concentrations (0, 15, 30, 45, 60, 75, and 90 μg mL−1), and the cells were incubated for 24 h. After incubation, the medium was replaced with MTT reagents and incubated for 3 h. Finally, the absorbance was measured using the microplate reader at 570 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular sensing of Hg2+ using GNNCs in A549 cells
ce:section.ce:para: Cells were seeded in the six-well plate with coverslips in essential medium and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C with 5% CO2. After incubation, the medium was removed, and the cells were washed with Dulbecco’s phosphate-buffered saline (DPBS) solution to remove the residual medium. GNNCs (50 μg mL−1) were added to the cells to use as the control. Different concentrations (2.5 and 5 μM) of Hg2+ ions were mixed with GNNCs and added to the cells before incubating in a 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C for 1 h. The coverslips were washed with DPBS three times and the cell images were processed using the fluorescence microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bio-imaging of shrimps and A549 cells
ce:section.ce:para: The shrimps were cleansed with DI water to remove dust particles. Then shrimps were incubated in DI water at room temperature. Thereafter, GNNCs (30 µL) were injected into the shrimps using a 1 mL syringe. To overcome the issues of possible background interferences (i.e. proteins and amino acids) from the shrimp, we have employed the fluorescence imaging for individual body parts such as head, body, and tail of shrimp instead of whole animal at a time. Finally, fluorescence images of head, body, and tail of shrimp were processed by employing a laser scanning confocal fluorescence microscope. Furtherly the A549 cells were cultured and incubated with GNNCs for 24 h and images were obtained by using confocal microscopy.

GNNC morphology was characterized using TEM, HRTEM, and SEM. Fig. 1 a shows the TEM images of as-prepared GNNCs. The GNNCs were spherical in shape and well dispersed with an average size of 4 nm. The inset shows the corresponding Gaussian fitting curve for size distribution histogram. Fig. 1b shows the HRTEM images of GNNCs with lattice fringes of 0.24 nm attributed to the (1 1 1) lattice plane of the face-centered cubic (fcc) structure of Au. Fig. 1c shows the magnified image of the nanoclusters, and the inset shows the Fast Fourier Transformation (FFT) pattern that correlates with the crystalline nature of the GNNCs. The phase purity of GNNCs was confirmed using XRD analysis, which demonstrated the diffraction angles of 38.8° and 45.6° attributed to the lattice planes of (1 1 1) and (2 0 0), respectively (Fig. 1d). Further elemental analysis of GNNCs was performed using SEM-EDX. Fig. 2 a-g shows the elemental mapping of carbon (C), oxygen (O), sodium (Na), chlorine (Cl), gold (Au), nitrogen (N), and sulfur (S), respectively. Fig. 2h represents the SEM image of GNNCs. The EDX spectrum for GNNCs is shown in Fig. S1, which shows that naringin is well attached to gold in GNNCs. The zeta potential (ζ) of as-prepared GNNCs was measured as −18.56 mV. After the addition of 1 and 5 µM Hg2+, the surface charge of GNNCs changed to −18.34 and −15.79 mV, respectively (Fig. S2), indicating that Hg2+ bound well to the surface of the GNNCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization and functional group analysis of GNNCs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 a shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of naringin (black) and GNNCs. The characteristic absorption band of naringin at 224 and 283 nm represents A-ring (flavonols, benzoyl system of the band II) and B-ring (flavones, cinnamoyl system of the band I), respectively. The absorptions were red shifted to 226 and 284 nm after forming GNNCs. No plasmonic absorption peak was observed as in gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) around 520 nm. Insets (i) and (ii) represent the image of GNNCs under daylight and UV light, respectively. From the TEM and UV–Vis absorption spectra results, we believe that formation of large size (≈4 nm) and green color emissive Au NCs may attributed to the addition of naringin to the GSH-BSA/gold solution. Moreover, surface plasmonic absorption peak was also not observed in the GNNCs under the visible region (520 nm). This clearly distinguishes the formed material as Au NCs, but not Au NPs [44]. These results are well-supported with the previously published report [45]. Fig. 3b shows the PL excitation spectra (black, λem = 514 nm) and emission spectra (red, λex = 394 nm) of GNNCs. The emission maximum of GNNCs was observed at 514 nm. Fig. 3c shows the excitation-dependent emission behavior of GNNCs with excitation wavelength varied from 300 to 500 nm, which is consistent with previous reports [46,47]. Fig. 3d shows the FT-IR spectra of L-GSH (i), naringin (ii), GNNCs (iii), and GNNCs treated with Hg2+ ions (iv). In curve i, the absorption bands at 1398 (asymmetric COO−), 1712 (symmetric COO−), and 1241 cm−1 (bending vibrations of OH) are due to the COOH group of GSH. The characteristic peak at 2522 cm−1 is attributed to SH stretching vibration, and the peak at 3170–3349 cm−1 is due to (NH2) groups. In curve ii, the peaks at 1081, 1637, and 2923 cm−1 are attributed to CO, CO, and CH stretching groups, respectively. The peaks at 3286, 3367, and 3374 cm−1 are attributed to OH aromatic stretching vibrations of naringin. Interestingly, after the formation of GNNCs, these characteristic OH groups of naringin were blue shifted to 3280 cm−1. Based on the UV–Vis and FT-IR spectroscopy investigations, we believe that the polyphenols (OH groups) of naringin also involved in the formation of gold nanoclusters (Fig. S3). After forming the GNNCs, the peaks representing the SH group were blue shifted to a wavenumber of 2360 cm−1 (curve iii). Interestingly, the SH group of GNNCs nearly disappeared after binding with Hg2+ (black circle in curve iv), which may be due to the SH group interaction with Hg2+ ions. These results were well agreeing with the previous reports [48]. Fig. 4 a shows the total X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectra of GNNCs before (i) and after (ii) treatment with Hg2+ ions, and b-h show high-resolution spectra of C 1s, O1s, N 1s, S 2p, Cl 2p, Au 4f, and Hg 4f, respectively. Fig. 4a revealed that GNNCs have the elements including C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, S 2p, Cl 2p, and Au 4f with corresponding binding energies of 285.23, 532.99, 400.61, 201.17, and 85.7 eV, respectively (curve i). After treating with Hg2+, a new peak for Hg 4f was observed with a binding energy of 100.23 eV and slightly shifted binding energies in other elements (curve ii). Fig. 4b-g shows the deconvoluted individual high-resolution XPS spectra. The deconvolutions of the C 1s show the presence of four peaks, which were attributed to CC, COC, CO, and OCO groups at 284.78, 285.98, 287.18, and 288.78 eV, respectively (b, curve i). The deconvolutions of the C 1s show three peaks at 284.98, 286.78, and 288.98 eV, after Hg2+ treatment (b, curve ii). The deconvolutions of O 1s (c) and N 1s (d) demonstrated the binding energies of 532.78, 532.88 eV and 400.58, 399.98 eV, before and after treatment with Hg+. The deconvolutions of S 2p shows three peaks at 164.48, 165.78, and 168.2 eV corresponding to S2p3/2, S-H, and S 2p1/2, respectively (e, curve i). However, the S-H peak disappeared and the peaks of S2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 were blue-shifted to 164.28 and 167.08 eV, respectively, after treating with Hg2+ (curve ii). The high-resolution spectrum of Cl 2p (f) had three peaks at 198.88, 200.98, and 202.88 eV (curve i) and two peaks (curve ii) at 199.08 and 201.08 eV (Cl 2p3/2, Cl 2p1/2). The deconvolution of Au 4f (g) revealed two peaks at 85.68 and 89.38 eV, which were attributed to Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2, respectively (curve i). Significantly, four peaks appeared (curve ii) at 84.48 and 88.78 eV, responsible for metallic gold (Au0), and 86.88 and 91.08 eV, responsible for ionic gold (Au+) after the addition of Hg2+ [49,50] . The high-resolution spectrum of mercury (h) shows energy levels of Hg 4f7/2 and Hg 4f5/2 at 100.28 and 104.18 eV, respectively, indicating the metallic structure of mercury (Hg0). The FTIR and XPS results confirm the GNNCs formation and their binding affinity for Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL dependence on pH and long-term PL stability of GNNCs
ce:section.ce:para: The PL dependence of GNNCs on pH was studied. At acidic conditions, PL intensity of GNNCs increases gradually with increased pH. In basic conditions, the PL intensity was decreased. This effect may be due to the hydroxyl and thiol functional groups on the surface of GNNCs. The hydroxyl and thiol groups from naringin and GSH can be protonated in acidic conditions and deprotonated in basic conditions [51,52]. Interestingly, GNNCs showed the highest PL intensity at the physiological pH 7.2 (Fig. S4). The PL intensity of GNNCs was maintained up to 95% in the one-month period with intervals of 5 days. The green color emission of the GNNCs was also maintained (Fig. S5). The photostability of GNNCs was examined using continuous illumination with a xenon lamp for 1 h. The PL intensity was measured every 5 min, and no severe photo-bleaching was observed (Fig. S6). In addition, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed to study the thermal stability of GNNCs in the temperature range of 20–700 °C. The TGA plot (Fig. S7) shows that GNNCs decompose in four-steps with various temperature ranges. Table S1 lists the temperature range together with weight loss percentage. The first step shows an initial weight loss at 20–125.78 °C (7.025%) due to water loss. In the second and third steps, weight loss at 125.78–236.66 °C (14.68%) and at 236.66–410.38 °C (31.40%) was observed. This is due to decomposition of moieties like amine (NH2), thiol (SH), chlorine (Cl), sulfur (S), and other oxygen-based functional groups. In the final step, weight loss at 410.38–700 °C (9.366%) was recorded. The total weight loss of GNNCs was found to be 62.47% at 700 °C (Fig. S7) [53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity studies of GNNCs with other metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of the GNNCs was analyzed by measuring PL intensity with various concentrations of Hg2+ ranging from 0 to 5 µM as illustrated in Fig. 5 a. A good linear relationship between (F0-F/F0) with Hg2+ concentration was examined with in the concentration range of 0–0.75 µM with a correlation coefficient of (R2 = 0.999) (Fig. 5b). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 26.42 nM for Hg2+ detection as per 3σ/K. This sensitivity is good enough to detect a Hg2+ concentration of 30 nM which is allowed in drinking water according to WHO (World Health Organization) regulations [54]. The thiol (SH) functional groups of GNNCs may enhance the sensing ability toward Hg2+. The selectivity was tested by measuring PL intensity change with Hg2+ and other metal ions including Cd2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Co2+, K+, Cs2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Cu2+ and Al3+. For other metal ions, a higher concentration (50 μM) was used compared to Hg2+ (5 μM). Interestingly, 90% of PL quenching was observed with Hg2+, but negligible PL quenching was observed with other metals as shown in Fig. 5c. Inset image shows the fluorescence intensity photographs of all interferants (labeled in alphabetical order from a-o). Further, to confirm the selectivity for the detection of Hg2+, the changes in fluorescence intensity of GNNCs in the presence of Hg2+ mixed with other metal ions were investigated (Fig. S8). It is confirmed that fluorescence intensity of GNNCs can be quenched significantly by Hg2+ even in the presence of other metal ions. Noteworthy, as compared to control, the quenching efficiency was not much influenced by the existence of the other metal ions. This proves the high selectivity and potentiality of GNNCs towards Hg2+ detection. These results revealed that GNNCs possess remarkable selectivity toward Hg2+. The high selectivity can be attributed to the interaction between Hg2+ and SH functional groups, which facilitate the non-radiativerecombinationof the excitons via electron transfer. This agrees with the concept of Pearson’s HSAB principle, which suggests that soft acids always have preferable binding tendency with soft bases [55,56]. The above results demonstrate that GNNCs can be used as sensing media for Hg2+ with high sensitivity and selectivity with broad linear range of concentrations. This study and other previous reported Hg2+ sensing systems are compared in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Salt and oxidative effects of GNNCs
ce:section.ce:para: The salt effect of GNNCs was examined in NaCl solutions at various concentrations from 0 to 2 mol L−1 (Fig. 5d). However, remarkable PL quenching was not observed in GNNCs, suggesting that GNNCs are highly stable in various ionic strengths and can be used under physiological conditions [36]. Oxidative resistance of GNNCs was explored using different concentrations of H2O2 in the range of 0–1 mM at intervals of 0.1 mM. However, no significant change in GNNC PL intensity was observed (Fig. S9). This effect suggests that GNNCs have excellent stability even in oxidizing environments [53].

The fluorescence lifetime decay of GNNCs in the presence of Hg2+ is illustrated in Fig. S10. The fluorescence decay curves were fitted using the double exponential function as follows:
(1)
D
t
=
∑
i = 1
n
a i
e x p
-
t
τ i
where D denotes the PL decay and τi and ai represent PL lifetime and corresponding pre-exponential factors, respectively. The GNNCs demonstrate lifetimes of τ1 = 12.45 ns and τ2 = 0.028 ns. After adding Hg2+, the lifetimes were decreased with τ1 and τ2 of 10.32 and 0.024 ns, respectively. This suggests that fluorescence quenching occurs via the dynamic electron transfer from GNNCs to Hg2+ [57]. Further studies with XPS and FT-IR spectroscopy were performed to investigate the spectral changes after adding Hg2+ to the GNNCs. The disappearance of -SH functional group from the GNNCs after adding Hg2+ confirms that Hg2+ ions were tightly bound to the SH functional groups. Interestingly, XPS analysis shows that the atomic percentage of sulfur (S) was 1.16 in the GNNCs before treating with Hg2+. However, after binding Hg2+ to the GNNCs, the atomic percentage of the Hg and S were found to be 0.23 and 0.58, respectively. The total survey XPS spectra of GNNCs after adding Hg2+ shows a new peak with the binding energy of 100.23 eV (Fig. 4a, line ii), which is attributed to the binding energy of Hg 4f. In addition, the binding energy of the S 2p was shifted from 164.48 eV to 164.28 eV (Fig. 4e, line ii). These results support the excellent binding of SH groups in GNNCs with Hg2+ [58,59]. Based on these findings, we propose a plausible PL quenching mechanism for Hg2+ sensing. It involves the main binding site on the GNNCs, the SH group, originating from GSH, and it facilitates the binding of GNNCs with Hg2 (Fig. 6 ).

The cytotoxicity of GNNCs was investigated using the MTT assay in A549 and WI-38 cells. Fig. S11 shows the cytotoxic effects of GNNCs against A549 cells. The cell viability decreases with increased GNNC concentrations. This indicates that the GNNCs have a cytotoxic effect on cancer cells. Fig. S12 shows that the viability of the lung normal cells (WI-38) was maintained at a high level even with high GNNC concentrations. This means that GNNCs are biocompatible and can be used to sense Hg2+ ions in living cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular sensing of Hg2+ and in vitro imaging using GNNCs
ce:section.ce:para: The intracellular sensing of Hg2+ was performed using GNNCs as the fluorescent probes in A549 cells under the fluorescence microscope. The GNNCs demonstrated excellent multi-color imaging and intracellular sensing properties in A549 cells. Fig. 7 A shows the images of GNNCs-injected A549 cells without Hg2+ as control. After adding Hg2+ (2.5 and 5 µM) to the GNNCs, the fluorescence intensity of the cells decreased, as shown in Fig. 7B, C. The fluorescent area was clearly observed in the cytoplasm of A549 cells. Fig. 8 demonstrates the cell-imaging studies which were investigated using different optical filters at excitation wavelengths of 405 nm (blue), 488 nm (green), and 543 nm (red). This demonstrates that GNNCs can able to penetrate the cell. The above observations indicate that GNNCs can function in both intracellular sensing and imaging in living cells. Moreover, the multi-color imaging property of GNNCs was attributed to the excitation-dependent fluorescence emission behavior of the GNNCs [60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo fluorescence imaging of shrimp
ce:section.ce:para: Shrimp was used as an animal model for in vivo fluorescence imaging studies. The GNNCs (30 µL) were injected into body, head, and tail. Green-fluorescence emission of GNNCs also facilitate the imaging of tissues near the surface region with negligible autofluorescence interference. Finally, the multi-color (blue, green, and red) fluorescence images were obtained as illustrated in Fig. 9 using a confocal laser scanning microscope. Fig. 10 shows the photographs of GNNC-injected shrimps with or without Hg2+ treatment under UV illumination. Fig. 10A shows the green color area where GNNCs were injected, and the green color disappeared in Fig. 10B due to the fluorescence quenching of GNNCs by Hg2+. These results suggest that GNNCs are suitable fluorescent imaging agents for in vivo models.

In conclusion, we synthesized novel green color emissive GNNCs that can be used as versatile fluorescent probes for the selective Hg2+ detection. The HRTEM analysis revealed the average size of GNNCs to be 4 nm with good dispersion. The GNNCs showed a detection limit of 26.42 nM for the sensing of Hg2+ in a concentration range of 0–5 µM. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of GNNCs was further explained using XPS and FT-IR techniques. The GNNCs demonstrated excitation-dependent emission effects with a quantum yield of 13%. The GNNCs were successfully used as fluorescent imaging agents in shrimp. An excellent intracellular fluorescence sensing of Hg2+ was possible in A549 cells. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first investigation on the synthesis and application of gold-naringin nanoclusters in both bio-imaging and intracellular fluorescence sensing of Hg2+. GNNCs evolved as a magnificent dual-functional nanomaterial with multi-color imaging and intracellular sensing properties besides excellent selectivity, high photostability, and biocompatibility features. By comparing to the previously reported Au NCs [20,31], this work has exhibited potential sensing and imaging properties. Our present strategy provides a hopeful perspective in terms of sensitivity and selectivity towards Hg2+ detection and their imaging in cancer cells and shrimps. Further, these advantages facilitate the GNNCs as ideal fluorescence nanomaterials in the sensing and biomedical disciplines in the future.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the Smart Materials Research Center for IoT at Gachon University for its instrumental support (FT-IR, UV-Vis spectroscopy, and SEM). This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF2017R1D1A1B04035070) and by the Human Resource Development of the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) and the Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy (MOTIE) of the Republic of Korea (20174030201530).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Information regarding the characterization and real sample analysis are available in the supplementary information. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.145417.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Actuator B-Chem. 202 (2014) 741-747.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Venkateswarlu, B. Viswanath, A.S. Reddy, M. Yoon, Fungus-derived photoluminescent carbon nanodots for ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ions and photoinduced bactericidal activity, Sens. Actuator B-Chem. 258 (2018) 172-183.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thioether-based fluorescent covalent organic framework for selective detection and facile removal of mercury(II)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.Y. Ding, M. Dong, Y.W. Wang, Y.T. Chen, H.Z. Wang, C.Y. Su, W. Wang, Thioether-based fluorescent covalent organic framework for selective detection and facile removal of mercury(II), J. Am. Chem. Soc. 138 (2016) 3031-3037.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly photoluminescent carbon dots-based fluorescent chemosensors for sensitive and selective detection of mercury ions and application of imaging in living cells
